opp.name: Valiram P2P 2026

items.quote: Demo workshop completed 10 June 2026 per Next Step; no feedback logged, no confirmation of next-phase user validation
CPO committed to send sample BOQ template for renovation on 29 May call; no record of receipt

metrics.narrative: The CPO named the business problem on the 29 May 2026 call: Valiram is in rapid expansion mode (700+ stores across 9 countries, growing to 10), requiring faster tender processes and approvals for store renovations to keep pace. The CPO emphasized 'we are growing rapidly, expanding quite quite faster' and 'we are in the rapid store expansion mode... we are looking for a tender solution primarily that is probably AI driven for complex BOQ that can help us to drive the tender process faster and at the same time, conclude and then you know, get approval in the in the fast paced manner.' Additional operational pain: manual tender evaluation, lack of automated vendor screening (ESG, financial, media exposure, HR conflict-of-interest checks), and split-purchase-order compliance leakage. No quantified savings, cycle-time reduction, or cost impact was stated. Making the business case quantitative (e.g. tender-cycle days saved, compliance risk reduced, manual-effort hours eliminated) is the next step to build ROI and accelerate EB sign-off.

sources: Avoma discovery call 29 May 2026, CPO (speaker 1); SF Next Step 10 June 2026
champion.narrative: The CPO (Head of Procurement, Governance & Indirects, 30 years procurement experience, previously Sime Darby) is the emerging champion. On the 29 May call the CPO drove scope definition, set evaluation criteria in detail, and demanded a configured use-case demo ('If I don't see that, then there will be no further conversation'). The CPO also controlled next-phase sequencing ('we will put you to the next phase where we will let you interact with the user itself') and set the timeline (RFI now, close evaluation Q4 2026, deploy early 2027). The Procurement Manager (Michelle) was present but spoke minimally. Evidence of advocacy: the CPO granted a 1.5-hour demo workshop (10 June 2026) and provided detailed configuration requirements (brand/renovation BOQ templates, vendor screening via SSM/CTOS, split-PO detection scenarios) to enable a tailored demo. What is still missing: (a) access to the Economic Buyer or C-level sponsor (no introduction offered yet); (b) multi-threading beyond procurement (finance, IT, end-user stakeholders not yet engaged); (c) competitive intelligence (the CPO has not shared the RFI shortlist or named other vendors under evaluation); (d) post-demo feedback (no signal yet on whether the 10 June workshop met the stated criteria or what the next step is). Current engagement: active as of 10 June demo; 16 days since, awaiting the CPO's next move. The champion is developing but not yet at the level of internal advocate or multi-thread facilitator.

sources: Avoma discovery call 29 May 2026; SF Competitors__c (empty)
competition.narrative: No competitor named in any source. The CPO stated on 29 May 2026 'we are still now we are doing our so called RFI... just looking through what are the available solution in the market at the moment, in particular, for those that is more AI driven', confirming an active RFI with multiple vendors under evaluation, but did not name them. The Competitors__c Salesforce field is empty, and no rival was mentioned in the discovery call, Next Step, or tasks. The CPO's emphasis on AI-driven automation, BOQ template configurability, and automated vendor screening suggests the shortlist likely includes procurement-suite vendors with AI capabilities (e.g. Coupa, SAP Ariba, Jaggaer, GEP, Ivalua). The wedge: Zycus Merlin agentic AI platform with 100+ autonomous agents (highlighted on the 29 May call), SSM/CTOS integration for Malaysia-specific vendor screening, and configurable BOQ tender templates for retail/renovation use cases. The next move is to ask the CPO directly: 'Who else are you looking at in this RFI, and how can we help you benchmark us against them?' This is a critical knowledge gap that lowers deal predictability.

identify_pain.narrative: The CPO articulated four operational pains on the 29 May 2026 call, all tied to rapid store expansion (700+ stores across 9 countries, growing to 10). (1) Manual, slow tender processes for store renovations: 'we are looking for a tender solution primarily that is probably AI driven for complex BOQ that can help us to drive the tender process faster and at the same time, conclude and then you know, get approval in the in the fast paced manner.' The CPO emphasized the need for brand-specific and generic BOQ templates that can be quickly configured and reused. (2) Manual vendor evaluation with no automated scoring: the CPO requested a one-page technical/commercial matrix with AI-recommended vendor selection and rationale, to 'fast track our approval in terms of getting the our directors to take a quick look, and at one glance, these are the critical indicator that which vendor should be be awarded.' (3) Vendor risk exposure: no automated ESG, financial, media, or conflict-of-interest screening; the CPO wanted real-time high/medium/low risk alerts on vendor registration. (4) Split-purchase-order compliance leakage: 'we have a issues on split purchase order... I would like to see in particular how Zycus solutions can help to detect all the potential... split the chase order.' The pain owner is the CPO (Head of Procurement, Governance & Indirects), who owns tender process, vendor management, and compliance. Compelling event: aggressive expansion timeline and the need to shortlist a solution by Q4 2026 for early 2027 go-live.

sources: Avoma discovery call 29 May 2026; SF Products field (iSupplier, eProcurement, eInvoicing, Agentic AI)
paper_process.narrative: SaaS deployment model confirmed on the 29 May call; the CPO asked 'where is your server?' and George confirmed Singapore data center (details to be shared separately for confidentiality). The CPO requested data residency and infrastructure details to assess compliance. Integration requirements: Infor ERP for finance and in-house PO system for P2P; e-invoicing and validation workflows to link seamlessly. The CPO also required SSM (Companies Commission of Malaysia) and CTOS (credit bureau) integration for automated vendor screening, and HR-database integration for conflict-of-interest checks. No discussion yet of MSA/SOW, legal review, InfoSec questionnaire, or procurement channel (direct vs. partner). No mention of implementation partner or SI involvement. Current contract expiration date (if replacing an incumbent) is unknown. The next step to create visibility is to ask whether Valiram has a standard vendor onboarding process (InfoSec, legal, procurement terms) and what the SLA is, and whether an SI or partner will be involved in deployment.

sources: Avoma discovery call 29 May 2026; SF Next Step; account contact roster
economic_buyer.narrative: No Economic Buyer identified. The discovery and demo workshop engaged the CPO (Head of Procurement) and Procurement Manager, both operational stakeholders. No CFO, Group CEO, or C-level sponsor has appeared in any call or activity log. The CPO stated the timeline ('we are aggressively looking for suitable solution now... earliest that we are looking at probably by early next year') and drove scope, but made no reference to budget authority, commercial approval chain, or pricing discussions. The 29 May call surfaced 'our directors' as the approval gate for tender awards, but did not name who controls the commercial decision or signs the Zycus contract. With a $150k deal and a Q4 2026 evaluation close, identifying and engaging the budget owner (likely Group CFO, Group CEO Ian Lim, or VP Finance Gwen Chong per the account contact roster) is critical. The next move is to ask the CPO who approves the Zycus investment and request an introduction.

sources: Avoma discovery call 29 May 2026, CPO (speaker 1); SF Next Step 10 June 2026
decision_process.narrative: The buyer is running a structured RFI process, per the 29 May call and Next Step log. The CPO stated 'we are still now we are doing our so called RFI... just looking through what are the available solution in the market at the moment, in particular, for those that is more AI driven... when I say tailor, that means that this is something that... can be configured to my requirements.' The intended sequence: RFI (current stage) → shortlist solutions → next-phase user validation with configured templates → close evaluation by Q4 2026 → deployment early 2027. The CPO controls vendor engagement and demo sequencing ('we will put you to the next phase where we will let you interact with the user itself and let the user to see the solution with the configured so called template'). The CPO referenced 'our directors' as the approval authority for tender awards but did not map the approval chain for the Zycus commercial decision (number of approvers, CFO/CEO sign-off, procurement committee). The call sequence shows single-thread engagement (CPO + Procurement Manager only); finance, IT, and C-level have not yet joined. The next gate is post-demo feedback and invitation to the next-phase user validation.

sources: Avoma discovery call 29 May 2026, CPO (speaker 1); Avoma notes 29 May 2026
decision_criteria.narrative: The CPO laid out explicit evaluation criteria on the 29 May 2026 call, repeated and detailed when scoping the demo workshop request. The criteria are: (1) AI-driven tender solution for complex BOQ with brand-specific and generic renovation templates, fast setup and modification; (2) automated technical and commercial scoring with AI-recommended vendor selection and a one-page summary showing why vendor A is superior and others are not; (3) historical base-price benchmarking against similar store projects to flag price variance; (4) automated vendor registration with ESG, financial exposure, and media screening via SSM and CTOS integration, plus HR-database cross-check to detect director-employee conflicts, with real-time high/medium/low risk alerts; (5) procure-to-pay capability to detect and prevent split purchase orders for compliance; (6) SaaS deployment with data residency assurance (Singapore data center) and confidentiality of infrastructure. The CPO stated 'I would like to see certain, you know, matrix from the technical and commercial... when the final score who the AI should be able to automatically recommend a vendor and what is the reason for the recommendation... If I don't see that, then there will be no further conversation.' The demo on 10 June 2026 was structured to validate these six criteria; no post-demo feedback is logged yet.

cro_panel.intro: One read per score, in language a CRO/CO can act on. We're behind on this one — the reads below say why and what would change it.

blocks.read: We're well behind — this one is cold.

ai_fit_signal.summary: The buyer is AI Hungry. The CPO explicitly stated 'we are looking for a tender solution primarily that is probably AI driven' and 'in particular, for those that is more AI driven that we are interested to see' when describing the RFI process. The CPO requested AI-based vendor recommendation with scoring rationale, and emphasized 'low manual-effort, end-to-end AI automation' as a core requirement. George presented Zycus Merlin agentic AI platform with 100+ autonomous agents, and the buyer engaged positively. No AIS score is logged in Salesforce, but call evidence confirms high AI appetite.

deal_movement.summary: Deal created and qualified 29 May 2026 (28 days ago). Discovery call same day with CPO and Procurement Manager established scope (AI-driven tender for BOQ, vendor screening, split-PO detection) and timeline (deploy early 2027). Demo workshop delivered 10 June 2026 (16 days ago) per Next Step log. No field-history activity, no completed tasks, and no buyer-facing activity logged since 10 June. LastActivityDate 29 May (28 days ago) reflects SF logging lag, not deal reality. Awaiting post-demo feedback and next-phase invitation.

Risk: if RFI drags or next-phase user validation is delayed, Q4 decision compresses into year-end holidays and budget freezes, pushing deployment into mid-2027.

items.detail: Single-thread risk: only the CPO and Procurement Manager engaged; no finance, IT, end-user, or C-level involvement. If the CPO leaves, is overruled, or deprioritizes, the deal stalls. Multi-threading to finance (VP Finance Gwen Chong), IT (AVP IT Harris Mutu), and the Economic Buyer (likely Group CEO Ian Lim or CFO) is critical.

items.detail: Competitive blind spot: buyer is running an active RFI but has not named other vendors under evaluation. Zycus is operating without competitive intelligence, unable to differentiate proactively or counter rival positioning.

Champion strength will be confirmed or weakened by the CPO's response to the follow-up in the next 3-5 business days.

items: Positive: CPO set detailed evaluation criteria and emphasized 'If I don't see that, then there will be no further conversation', signaling serious intent and high bar (29 May 2026).
Positive: Buyer granted 1.5-hour on-site demo workshop (10 June 2026) and provided configuration requirements (BOQ template, vendor screening scenarios, split-PO detection).
Positive: CPO stated aggressive timeline ('we are aggressively looking for suitable solution now... hopefully, we can plan on one') and target deployment early 2027.
Neutral: 16 days since demo (10 June) with no logged post-demo feedback or next-phase confirmation; LastActivityDate 29 May reflects SF logging lag.
Negative: No multi-thread expansion beyond CPO and Procurement Manager; no finance, IT, or C-level engagement yet.
Negative: No competitive intelligence shared; buyer has not named RFI shortlist or other vendors under evaluation.
confidence_signals.summary: Mixed signals. Positive: buyer set clear evaluation criteria, granted 1.5-hour demo workshop, and expressed urgency ('we are aggressively looking for suitable solution now'). Neutral to negative: 16 days since demo with no logged feedback, no next-phase invitation, no multi-thread expansion, and no competitive intelligence shared. The deal is live (demo delivered 10 June) but awaiting the CPO's next move. Cooling risk is moderate if no response in the next 5-7 business days.

north_star_verdict.math: Created 29 May 2026 (28 days ago), qualified same day; 28 days in Qualified stage. Close date 31 Jan 2027 is 219 days out. LastActivityDate 29 May (28 days ago). Next Step logs demo workshop completed 10 June 2026 (16 days ago) to the CPO and Procurement Manager. No forward slip. Pace is appropriate for an early-stage enterprise RFI with a target go-live of early 2027 and a 7-month runway.
evidence: Discovery call 29 May 2026 established scope: AI-driven tender solution for complex BOQ, automated vendor screening (ESG/financial/media/HR conflict-check), and P2P split-order detection to support 700+ store expansion across 9 countries.

CPO articulated clear pain: rapid store roll-out (renovation tenders with brand-specific and generic BOQ templates), manual tender evaluation, lack of automated vendor risk scoring, and split-PO compliance leakage.
Next Step confirms demo workshop delivered 10 June 2026 (16 days ago) to the CPO and Procurement Manager; RFI stage active.

Buyer requested specific use-case demo: configure complex BOQ template by brand and renovation type, auto-score technical/commercial, AI-recommended vendor selection with rationale, historical base-price benchmarking, vendor screening via SSM/CTOS integration, HR-database conflict-check, and split-PO detection.
Buyer set timeline: complete RFI/evaluation by Q4 2026, target deployment early 2027.
28-day gap since LastActivityDate is within normal cadence for early-stage RFI; no evidence of stall or cooling.
north_star_verdict.headline: Early-stage RFI with 28-day silence post-demo workshop. Single-threaded to CPO/Procurement; competitive shortlist unmapped despite active multi-vendor evaluation. No follow-up milestone scheduled to advance Q4 2026 RFI completion target.

best_practice_check.summary: Three high-leverage levers to accelerate this RFI-stage deal toward shortlist and commercial discussions.

competitive_position.summary: No competitor identified. The buyer is running an active RFI ('we are still now we are doing our so called RFI... just looking through what are the available solution in the market') but has not shared the shortlist. This is a critical intelligence gap. Zycus positioning: Merlin agentic AI platform with 100+ autonomous agents, SSM/CTOS Malaysia-specific vendor screening integration, configurable BOQ tender templates for retail renovation use cases, and 25 years in market with 2,000 deployments globally. The wedge is AI-driven automation and localized compliance integration; the activation is to surface the RFI shortlist and run a competitive bake-off on the stated criteria (AI vendor recommendation, historical price benchmarking, automated risk scoring).

deltas.subject: Buyer target: close RFI evaluation by Q4 2026, deploy early 2027. Close date is 31 Jan 2027 (219 days out), aligned with buyer timeline. Risk: if RFI drags or next-phase user validation is delayed, Q4 decision compresses into year-end holidays and budget freezes, pushing deployment into mid-2027.

deltas.subject: Single-thread risk: only the CPO and Procurement Manager engaged; no finance, IT, end-user, or C-level involvement. If the CPO leaves, is overruled, or deprioritizes, the deal stalls. Multi-threading to finance (VP Finance Gwen Chong), IT (AVP IT Harris Mutu), and the Economic Buyer (likely Group CEO Ian Lim or CFO) is critical.

deltas.subject: Competitive blind spot: buyer is running an active RFI but has not named other vendors under evaluation. Zycus is operating without competitive intelligence, unable to differentiate proactively or counter rival positioning.

deltas.to: Capture post-demo feedback immediately and convert momentum into next-phase user validation. The 10 June workshop was scoped to validate six explicit criteria (
deltas.subject: Capture post-demo feedback immediately and convert momentum into next-phase user validation. The 10 June workshop was scoped to validate six explicit criteria (AI BOQ tender, auto-scoring, price benchmarking, vendor screening, split-PO detection, data residency). The CPO stated 'If I don't see that, then there will be no further conversation.' It has been 16 days since the demo with no logged feedback. The highest-leverage move is to re-engage the CPO directly (email + follow-up call within 3 business days) to confirm: (a) did the demo meet the stated criteria, (b) what gaps or questions remain, (c) when is the next-phase user validation, and (d) who else should join (finance, IT, end-users). This is the single most critical action to maintain RFI momentum and avoid silent elimination.

deltas.to: Map and engage the Economic Buyer before commercials begin. The CPO is operational; no CFO, Group CEO, or C-level sponsor has appeared. With a $150k investment
deltas.subject: Map and engage the Economic Buyer before commercials begin. The CPO is operational; no CFO, Group CEO, or C-level sponsor has appeared. With a $150k investment and a Q4 2026 decision target, budget authority must be identified and brought into the conversation before pricing. Ask the CPO directly: 'Who approves the Zycus investment on your side—CFO, Group CEO, or procurement committee—and can you introduce us?' The account contact roster names Group CEO Ian Lim and VP Finance Gwen Chong as likely EB candidates. An executive-connect play (deal owner's manager Carl Kimball joins) is appropriate once the EB is named and the deal reaches shortlist stage.

deltas.to: Surface the competitive RFI shortlist and run a criteria-based bake-off. The CPO confirmed an active RFI but has not named other vendors. This is a blind-spot r
deltas.subject: Surface the competitive RFI shortlist and run a criteria-based bake-off. The CPO confirmed an active RFI but has not named other vendors. This is a blind-spot risk: if Coupa, Ariba, GEP, or Jaggaer are in play, Zycus needs to know their positioning and differentiate on the six stated criteria (especially AI vendor recommendation, SSM/CTOS integration, and BOQ configurability). Ask the CPO: 'Who else are you evaluating in this RFI, and how can we help you benchmark us against them on your criteria?' Offer a side-by-side scorecard or reference-customer call with a retail/renovation use case to reinforce the wedge.

value.flag: Capture post-demo feedback immediately and convert momentum into next-phase user validation. The 10 June workshop was scoped to validate six explicit criteria (AI BOQ tender, auto-scoring, price benchmarking, vendor screening, split-PO detection, data residency). The CPO stated 'If I don't see that, then there will be no further conversation.' It has been 16 days since the demo with no logged feedback. The highest-leverage move is to re-engage the CPO directly (email + follow-up call within 3 business days) to confirm: (a) did the demo meet the stated criteria, (b) what gaps or questions remain, (c) when is the next-phase user validation, and (d) who else should join (finance, IT, end-users). This is the single most critical action to maintain RFI momentum and avoid silent elimination.
